front: Work: written in terza rima -- stanzas of 11-syllable lines where the second line rhymes with the first and third of the next stanza
Work: opens 'Midway through life's journey'; the narrator encounters a lion, a leopard, and a she-wolf in 'a forest dark'
Work: Canto V -- Francesca da Rimini shares an adulterous kiss while reading about 'Lancelot, how Love did him enthrall'
Work: Canto XXVI -- Ulysses declares 'you were made men / To follow after knowledge and excellence'
Work: on Mars (fifth sphere of paradise), the poet meets great-great-grandfather Cacciaguida; souls form a Greek cross
Work: first circle of Hell is Limbo, unusually populated with virtuous pagans and apathetic souls
Work: Gustave Dore illustrated it; shows Phlegyas rowing across the Styx past the city of Dis in Canto VIII
Work: Rodin's Gates of Hell depicts scenes from it, including The Kiss, Ugolino and His Children, The Thinker (originally the poet), and The Three Shades
Work: Delacroix's Barque shows the poet cowering from figures chewing on a boat while crossing the Styx
Work: Bouguereau painted a scene of a nude man biting another's throat for the Prix de Rome
Work: Pasolini's Salo takes its section titles from this poem ('Circle of Blood,' 'Antinferno')
Work: early prosimetrum in the dolce stil novo; the character Love feeds the poet's heart to Beatrice in a vision and says 'Ego dominus tuus'
Work: begins by noting the 'incipit' in 'that part of the book of my memory'; Dante Gabriel Rossetti translated it in 1861
Work: unfinished philosophical work with Boethius-inspired praise for Lady Philosophy; refers to Eros as son of Venus
Author: T.S. Eliot obsession -- Prufrock opens with six-line quote from Inferno; Waste Land calls Pound 'the better craftsman'; Little Gidding in terza rima
Author: close friend of Guido Cavalcanti; meets Cavalcanti's father in the sixth circle of Hell
Author: Samuel Beckett wrote an essay titled for him, Bruno, Vico, and Joyce (periods indicate centuries between them)
Author: one of the 'three crowns' of Italian literature; Carducci said he 'looked outward' while Petrarch looked inward and Boccaccio upward
